title: What is Machine Learning?
bullets: ML is a subset of AI where systems learn from data without explicit programming
Three main paradigms: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning
Goal: enable computers to generalise from examples rather than following hand-coded rules
tldr: ML lets computers learn patterns from data automatically.

title: Supervised Learning
bullets: Trains on labeled data — each sample has an input X and a target output Y
Key algorithms: Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVMs
Used for tasks like spam detection, image classification, house price prediction
tldr: Supervised learning maps inputs to known outputs using labeled examples.

title: Unsupervised & Reinforcement Learning
bullets: Unsupervised: finds hidden structure in unlabeled data (e.g. K-Means, PCA)
Reinforcement Learning: agent learns via trial-and-error with reward/penalty signals
RL is behind game-playing AI (AlphaGo) and robotics
tldr: Unsupervised finds patterns; RL learns through interaction and feedback.

title: Training: Loss Functions & Optimisation
bullets: A model is a mathematical function mapping inputs → predictions
Loss function measures prediction error — MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification
Gradient Descent minimises loss by iteratively updating weights
Learning rate controls update step size — critical hyperparameter
tldr: Training adjusts model weights using gradient descent to minimise a loss function.

title: Overfitting, Underfitting & the Bias-Variance Tradeoff
bullets: Overfitting: great on train data, poor on new data (high variance)
Underfitting: model too simple to capture patterns (high bias)
Always split data into train / validation / test sets
Feature engineering transforms raw data into informative inputs
tldr: Balance bias and variance; use regularisation and proper data splits to build generalisable models.
